findings.title: Hero Has No Headline or Value Proposition

proposed_anchor.reason: A primary headline belongs in the hero band, immediately above the search input that currently anchors the top zone

evidence_anchors.context: The 1920x1080 hero band carries a dark photograph of a WRX-style car with no overlaid headline text. The only above-fold copy is the navigation labels (Shop All, Shop by Category, Shop by Vehicle), the search input placeholder (Search), and the small vehicle-picker prompt (SELECT YOUR VEHICLE / Car parts, simplified).

reference_citations.section: Finding 1: The 5-Second Test — Clarity Is the Foundational Hero Metric
Finding 4: Required Above-Fold Elements — Minimum Viable Hero

findings.observation: The hero band on awdmods.com presents a full-bleed photograph of a tuned car but renders zero headline copy. A first-time visitor sees a navigation strip, a search box, and a tiny SELECT YOUR VEHICLE link — no statement of what the store sells, who it serves, or why a buyer should choose AWDMods over a competitor. The 5-second test fails: a visitor would have to read the navigation labels and infer the category from the car photograph to understand this is a performance-parts store for AWD vehicles.
findings.recommendation: Add a single H1 headline overlaid on the hero photograph naming the buyer and the proof point — for example, Performance Parts for Subaru WRX, STI, Ford Focus RS and ST. Keep it 6-12 words, place it left of center over the darkest area of the photograph for contrast, and pair it with a one-line subheadline naming the differentiator (fitment-verified, free shipping over $75, in-house support). The current SELECT YOUR VEHICLE micro-link should sit beneath this headline, not replace it.
findings.why_this_matters: Cold paid traffic, organic search visitors landing on the homepage, and even returning shoppers form a yes-or-no decision in the first 5 seconds. Without a headline naming the audience and the value, this hero converts only visitors who already know the brand — every other arrival has to do the cognitive work of decoding the page from photograph and navigation alone, and most leave.

findings.title: No Primary CTA Button in Hero

proposed_anchor.reason: A primary CTA button belongs inside the hero band, beneath the missing headline

evidence_anchors.context: No filled, high-contrast button appears inside the hero photograph. The only above-fold actions are the search input (e23, 929x55), the SELECT YOUR VEHICLE link in the upper-right (no e_index), and the 5 blue category buttons that sit BELOW the hero photograph at y=746-786. The five SHOP buttons live inside the category-card row, not the hero.

reference_citations.section: Finding 9: Single CTA Focus — Whirlpool 42% documented
Finding 4: Required Above-Fold Elements — Minimum Viable Hero

findings.observation: The hero band offers no single dominant call-to-action. The vehicle picker (SELECT YOUR VEHICLE / Car parts, simplified) is rendered as small text in the top-right corner, easy to overlook against the busy photograph. The five category buttons (SHOP PERFORMANCE, SHOP HANDLING, SHOP INTERIOR, SHOP EXTERIOR, SHOP ELECTRONICS) compete equally with one another below the hero — no one of them is positioned as the page's primary commit.
findings.recommendation: If the vehicle picker is the intended primary action (it is the highest-leverage filter for fitment-driven parts), promote it: render Find Parts For My Car as a filled blue button at least 200x55 the same blue used on the category cards, placed under the new headline inside the hero photograph. Demote the five category-card buttons to a secondary row OR remove them from the immediate above-fold so the eye lands on a single conversion path.
findings.why_this_matters: Without a primary CTA, the hero leaves the visitor to choose from four competing entry points: type a search, click the small vehicle picker, scroll to a category card, or use the top-nav menu. Each added choice fragments attention and depresses the click-through rate of every option — the Whirlpool single-CTA test showed 42 percent lift from removing competing CTAs at exactly this commitment point.

findings.title: No Above-Fold Trust Signal

proposed_anchor.reason: A trust strip belongs immediately under the header banner, in the gap before the hero photograph

evidence_anchors.context: The above-fold band contains no aggregate review rating, no customer count, no fitment-guarantee copy, and no third-party trust marks. The thin top promotional bar reads FREE SHIPPING on most orders $75+ — a logistics offer, not a credibility signal. Star ratings only appear at scroll_y=1364 (e45/e46), well below the 1080 fold.

reference_citations.section: Finding 10: Trust Signal in Hero — The Single Minimum Viable Trust Element
Finding 4: 57% of Viewing Time Is Spent Above the Fold

findings.observation: The above-fold band carries no credibility marker — no aggregate star rating, no customer count, no Trustpilot or Google review badge, no Authorized Dealer call-out for the brands AWDMods carries, no fitment-guarantee statement. The first review evidence on the page is the 5.0 / 5.0 (2 reviews) marker on a featured product (e45) which only appears at scroll position 1364, below the fold. Visitors evaluating a high-consideration purchase (Borla cat-back exhausts at $1,549.99, AEM intakes at $403.40) form their first credibility judgment from the hero alone.
findings.recommendation: Insert a slim trust strip directly beneath the header, above the hero photograph. Three to four marks works: aggregate review rating with count (4.8 over 1,200+ reviews) sourced from the existing Yotpo or Shopify reviews integration, an Authorized Dealer line listing two or three of the marquee brands carried (Borla, AEM, COBB), a Fitment Guaranteed line, and an Easy Returns or 30-Day Return line. Keep the strip under 60 pixels tall so it does not push the hero photograph below the fold.
findings.why_this_matters: 57 percent of desktop viewing time is spent above the fold, so the credibility evidence the visitor sees there does most of the work of the page's trust posture. With zero trust signal in the hero, every visitor must scroll down through the categories and into the featured collection before they encounter the first proof point — a high-friction journey that price-sensitive performance-parts buyers will not complete.

findings.title: Five Equal-Weight Category CTAs Compete

evidence_anchors.context: Five SHOP X buttons (Performance, Handling, Interior, Exterior, Electronics) sit in a single row at y=746-786, each rendered identically: same blue fill, same width, same height, same uppercase white label. None is differentiated as the primary path; the squint test produces five equal blocks.

reference_citations.section: Finding 9: Single CTA Focus — Whirlpool 42% documented
Finding 3: Visual Hierarchy Squint Test — Five-Level Contrast Framework

findings.observation: The category-card row presents five identical filled-blue buttons with no visual hierarchy. Performance is almost certainly the highest-revenue category for an automotive performance store — intakes, exhaust, and turbo upgrades carry larger basket sizes and higher attach rates than Interior or Electronics — but its button reads at exactly the same visual weight as Electronics. The squint test confirms the issue: blur the row and five identical rectangles remain.
findings.recommendation: Either elevate one card as the lead path (larger card footprint, brighter accent, leading position with a Most Popular or Best Sellers tag on Performance) or, on a homepage, rank the five by revenue contribution and reduce the secondary categories to ghost or text-link styling. The merchandising team should pick one approach based on internal data; the current configuration is the worst case (five equal commits, none promoted).
findings.why_this_matters: When five conversion paths share equal visual weight, the visitor pays a small attention tax to scan all five and then makes a probabilistic choice — which usually means clicking nothing and continuing to scroll. A clear primary card concentrates traffic on the highest-margin path and reduces decision fatigue at the moment of intent.

findings.title: Top Promo Bar Buries Free Shipping Threshold

proposed_anchor.reason: The free-shipping threshold copy currently rendered in the very thin top promo bar should be promoted to a more visible trust position

evidence_anchors.context: FREE SHIPPING on most orders $75+ runs across the very top of the page in a thin blue bar with small white text — easy to dismiss as banner-blindness territory. The empty-cart drawer (e56 text) confirms the threshold (YOU'RE $75.00 AWAY FROM FREE SHIPPING) but visitors only see this after opening the cart.

reference_citations.section: Finding 6: Banner Blindness -- Users Ignore Ad-Like Content Across 3 Decades

findings.observation: The free-shipping threshold — a real and meaningful offer for a category where the typical $135-$1,500 SKU comfortably crosses the $75 minimum — is rendered in the page's least-attention zone: a thin promotional ribbon at the very top, in small text against a saturated blue bar that mimics the ad-banner format. Three decades of banner-blindness research show this format is reliably ignored.
findings.recommendation: Either move the free-shipping line into the new trust strip recommended in Finding 3 (placed beneath the header), so it earns above-fold reading attention, or pair it with a per-product progress indicator on the PDP. Keep the top promo ribbon for genuinely time-bounded promotions (Memorial Day Sale ends Monday) where the banner-style is appropriate.
findings.why_this_matters: Free-shipping thresholds drive average order value when visitors see them early enough to add a second item. Buried in banner-blindness real estate, the offer is doing none of that work — the visitor only encounters the threshold after they have already added one item and opened the cart.

findings.title: Search Input Dominates Above-Fold Real Estate

evidence_anchors.context: The header search input (e23) measures 929x55 pixels — nearly half the 1920px viewport width — and sits at the most prominent location in the header band. By contrast, the SELECT YOUR VEHICLE vehicle picker (the highest-intent action on a fitment-driven parts site) is rendered as small text in the upper-right corner.

reference_citations.section: Finding 5: 80% of Fixations Fall on the Left Half of the Page
Finding 3: Visual Hierarchy Squint Test — Five-Level Contrast Framework

findings.observation: The header allocates a 929-pixel-wide search bar to the center-left zone — the area that receives 80 percent of left-half fixations on desktop — while the vehicle-fitment picker (the action that filters the catalog to in-fitment parts and prevents return-rate-driving wrong-fitment purchases) is buried in the upper-right corner as small text. Search is a useful path for visitors who already know the part name, but vehicle selection is the higher-leverage entry for the larger audience that knows the car but not the SKU.
findings.recommendation: Either swap the dominance — promote the vehicle-fitment picker to a 600-700px wide visual block in the center of the header with the year/make/model selectors visible by default, and shrink the search to a smaller right-aligned input — or merge the two so the vehicle picker is the primary path and search becomes the secondary text-input within the same element. Test against the current configuration; the prior runs of similar fitment-first PDP redesigns on parts retailers show double-digit conversion lift.
findings.why_this_matters: On a fitment-driven parts catalog, wrong-fitment purchases drive returns, support tickets, and chargebacks — every order that ships against the wrong vehicle is a cost. Putting the fitment picker at low visual weight while the free-text search dominates inverts the friction: visitors who do not yet know the exact part name will type a guess, get unfiltered results, and either bounce or buy a wrong-fitment SKU.

notes: Hero photograph is high quality and on-brand (in-context shot of an aftermarket-modified WRX-style car) — supports the brand positioning even though no headline overlays it. Image type is correct per hero-section-psychology Finding 7.

Page-length and CTA cadence look acceptable for a homepage of this consideration level — three screenfuls (hero/categories, featured collection, newsletter/footer) with CTA opportunities in each. Not flagged as a finding.
